review_summary: Textual grounding is sufficient for concept-focused questions, and no separate video clip is needed.

question_bank.question: Why introduce sampling into open-ended decoding?
choices: To avoid always following a single highest-probability continuation.
To make the model ignore its next-token distribution.
To force every generated sequence to match a reference exactly.
To remove all randomness from generation.
question_bank.explanation: Open-ended text often has many valid continuations. Sampling lets the model choose among plausible options instead of always taking the same argmax path.

question_bank.question: Why can vanilla sampling from the full vocabulary produce bad tokens?
choices: Language distributions are heavy-tailed, so many low-probability bad tokens can have substantial total mass.
The top token always has probability zero.
The model cannot assign probabilities to rare tokens.
Vanilla sampling only chooses tokens not in the vocabulary.
question_bank.explanation: Even if each bad token is unlikely, there may be many of them. Their combined probability can be large enough that unconstrained sampling sometimes picks one.

question_bank.question: What does top-k sampling do?
choices: It samples only from the k highest-probability tokens after zeroing out the rest.
It always picks the kth most likely token.
It samples from all tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds k.
It reranks complete sequences using a separate classifier.
question_bank.explanation: Top-k truncates the next-token distribution to a fixed-size candidate set. This reduces tail risk but can be too restrictive or too permissive depending on the distribution.

question_bank.question: Why is top-p, or nucleus, sampling often more adaptive than fixed top-k sampling?
choices: It chooses the smallest candidate set whose cumulative probability mass passes a threshold.
It always uses exactly the same number of candidate tokens.
It bans the most likely token whenever the distribution is sharp.
It replaces probabilities with uniform scores across the vocabulary.
question_bank.explanation: Top-p adapts the number of options to the shape of the distribution. A sharp distribution may need few tokens, while a flatter distribution may need more viable candidates.

question_bank.question: How does temperature affect a next-token distribution?
choices: Higher temperature flattens the distribution, while lower temperature sharpens it toward greedy decoding.
Higher temperature removes all low-probability tokens from consideration.
Lower temperature makes every token equally likely.
Temperature changes only the length of the output, not token probabilities.
question_bank.explanation: Temperature rescales logits before softmax. Increasing it spreads probability mass across more tokens, while decreasing it concentrates mass on the most likely tokens.

question_bank.question: What is the basic idea of reranking sampled candidates?
choices: Generate multiple candidates, score them with quality criteria, and choose the best-scoring one.
Generate one token greedily and never revise the sequence.
Replace the language model with a fixed n-gram blocking rule.
Choose the shortest candidate regardless of quality.
question_bank.explanation: Reranking separates generation from selection. Sampling creates diverse candidates, and additional scoring functions can prefer outputs with better coherence, style, factuality, or consistency.
